name: Nannari Sarbath
description: Nannari Sarbath is a traditional South Indian cooling drink made from the roots of the Nannari plant (Indian Sarsaparilla). It is a natural body coolant, blood purifier, digestive aid and an excellent thirst quencher during scorching summers. The aromatic roots are boiled, reduced and sweetened into a syrup concentrate that can be stored and diluted with water and lemon juice whenever needed. It is also a key ingredient in the famous Madurai Jigarthanda.

steps.instruction: Crush nannari roots using a mortar and pestle to loosen them and break into 2 to 3 pieces.
Remove the white inner stick part from each root and discard it — it is not edible.
steps.tip: Always remove the white inner stick from nannari roots — it is not edible.

steps.instruction: Wash the roots 2 to 3 times by rubbing in water until all clay and dirt are removed.
Dry the roots until there is no moisture.
Transfer to a mixer jar and pulse a few times to make a coarse mixture.
Add 2 litres water to a mixing bowl. Add the nannari mixture and mix well.
Cover and let it rest for a minimum of 5 hours.
After 5 hours, transfer to a cooking pot and bring to a boil.
Cook on medium flame until the mixture reduces to half in volume.
Switch off, cool down, then strain using a double-folded cloth placed on a strainer.
steps.tip: Use a double-folded muslin cloth to strain and remove even fine root particles.

steps.instruction: Squeeze the cloth well to extract all the juice.
Return the strained liquid to the pot. Rinse the pot before adding.
Bring to a boil again. When it starts boiling, add 2 cups sugar.
Mix well and boil until the syrup is sticky and half-string consistency forms.
steps.tip: The syrup thickens slightly after cooling and storing, so switch off accordingly.

steps.instruction: Switch off. The syrup should be thick but still flowing — like honey.
Cool completely, then add juice from 1 big lemon. Mix well.
steps.tip: Lemon juice is added after cooling to prevent crystallization and increase shelf life.

steps.instruction: Store in a clean glass bottle. Keeps well at room temperature for a week, or in the fridge for 2 to 3 months.
In a glass, add ice cubes, 3 tbsp nannari syrup and lemon juice from ½ lemon.
Add 1 cup chilled water. Mix well and serve immediately.
steps.tip: Add soaked sabja seeds for extra cooling effect and texture.

tips: Always remove the white inner stick from nannari roots — it is not edible.
Use a double-folded muslin cloth to strain and remove even fine root particles.
Lemon juice is added after cooling to prevent crystallization and increase shelf life.
The syrup thickens slightly after cooling and storing, so switch off accordingly.
You can use jaggery instead of sugar for a healthier, caramel-flavoured version.
Store-bought nannari syrup can be used for convenience, but homemade tastes far superior.
Add soaked sabja seeds for extra cooling effect and texture.
You can make nannari popsicles by pouring the sarbath into moulds and freezing overnight.
